questions.question: Disqualification under s.839 CA 2014 differs fundamentally from restriction under s.819 in terms of both the grounds available and the consequence. Which of the following BEST captures this distinction?

options.A: Disqualification is automatic on insolvency; restriction requires proof of dishonesty
options.B: Restriction is a mandatory default regime triggered by insolvent winding up; disqualification is a discretionary court-imposed order available on broader grounds, including conduct in solvent companies, conviction of indictable offences, and persistent default
options.C: Disqualification bans a person from being a shareholder; restriction bans a person from being a director
options.D: Restriction applies for a maximum of three years; disqualification is always permanent

questions.explanation: Restriction under s.819 is triggered by insolvent winding up and applies unless the director displaces it. Disqualification under s.839 is discretionary, available on broader grounds (persistent default, indictable offence conviction, unfitness), applies to solvent and insolvent companies, and the period is set by the court. Disqualification imposes a complete ban without the capital escape route available in restriction.

questions.question: Under s.839(1) CA 2014, the court may make a disqualification order against a person on multiple grounds. Which of the following is a specified ground for disqualification under s.839(1)?

options.A: The person failed to attend more than 50% of board meetings in a given year
options.B: The person has been convicted on indictment of any indictable offence in relation to a company, or involving fraud, dishonesty, or breach of duty in relation to a company
options.C: The person is a director of a company that has been struck off the CRO register for failure to file an annual return
options.D: The person has been previously subject to a restriction order under s.819 more than once

questions.explanation: s.839(1)(a) CA 2014 provides that the court may disqualify a person who has been convicted on indictment of an indictable offence in relation to a company, or involving fraud, dishonesty, or breach of duty in connection with a company. This is one of the primary grounds for disqualification and reflects the seriousness of criminal conduct in the company law context.

questions.question: In Business Communications Ltd v Baxter [1995] (Ireland), the court made a disqualification order on the ground of 'unfitness'. What type of conduct satisfied the unfitness threshold in that case?

options.A: The director had failed to comply with a restriction order that had previously been made against them
options.B: The director had engaged in a systematic pattern of corporate governance failures: using companies as personal piggy banks, ignoring creditors, and persistent non-compliance with statutory requirements across multiple companies
options.C: The director had made a single fraudulent misrepresentation in a share prospectus, resulting in substantial investor losses
options.D: The director had acted as director of a foreign company that had been wound up in a foreign jurisdiction

questions.explanation: In Business Communications Ltd v Baxter [1995], the court disqualified the respondent based on a persistent and systematic pattern of conduct across multiple companies: treating company funds as personal funds, ignoring creditor obligations, and repeated non-compliance. The court looked at the totality of conduct across the respondent's corporate history.

questions.question: Under s.839(3) CA 2014, the court may make a disqualification order where a person has been persistently in default in relation to relevant requirements under the Companies Acts. How is 'persistent default' defined or evidenced?

options.A: Persistent default requires proof of at least ten separate defaults within a two-year period
options.B: Persistent default is established by showing that the person has three or more convictions for a relevant offence, or by showing a pattern of non-compliance with filing obligations even without formal convictions
options.C: Persistent default may be conclusively proved by showing that the person has been convicted of, or a default order has been made against them in respect of, not less than three relevant requirements within five years
options.D: Persistent default is only relevant where the defaults caused direct financial loss to creditors

questions.explanation: Under s.839(3) CA 2014, persistent default may be conclusively proved by showing that the person has been convicted of, or had a default order made against them in respect of, not less than three relevant obligations within a five-year period. This creates a presumption of disqualification-worthy default without requiring separate proof of a subjective pattern.

questions.question: The maximum period for a disqualification order under s.839 CA 2014 is set by the court. What is the maximum period the court may impose under the general disqualification provisions?

options.A: Five years — the same maximum as restriction orders under s.819
options.B: There is no fixed maximum — the court sets the period it considers appropriate having regard to the gravity of the conduct
options.C: Ten years for dishonesty-related grounds; five years for corporate governance failures
options.D: Fifteen years for indictable offence convictions; ten years for unfitness grounds

questions.explanation: Unlike restriction (which is fixed at five years under s.820), the court has discretion under s.839 to set the period of disqualification it considers appropriate. There is no statutory cap specified in s.839 itself for the general disqualification provision, allowing the court to impose whatever period justice requires.

questions.question: A person subject to a disqualification order under s.839 CA 2014 acts as a director of a company during the disqualification period. What are the legal consequences?

options.A: Their directorial acts are voidable but automatically valid until challenged by the liquidator
options.B: Acting as director while disqualified is a criminal offence, the person may be personally liable for the company's debts, and the directorial acts may be void
options.C: The only consequence is an administrative fine — the disqualification period is automatically extended by one year per violation
options.D: The disqualified person is personally liable only for debts incurred after they were notified of the disqualification order

questions.explanation: Under s.844 CA 2014, acting as a director while disqualified is a Category 2 criminal offence. Additionally, s.845 may impose personal liability on the disqualified person for company debts incurred during the period they acted in breach of the order. The consequences are significantly more severe than for breach of a restriction order.

questions.question: Section 842 CA 2014 grants the Corporate Enforcement Authority (CEA) a direct power in relation to disqualification. What does this power entail?

options.A: The CEA may issue disqualification orders administratively, without court involvement, for Category 1 and 2 offences
options.B: The CEA may apply directly to the court for disqualification orders in appropriate cases — it is not dependent on a liquidator making the application
options.C: The CEA may only apply for disqualification where it has first prosecuted the director for a criminal offence
options.D: The CEA may issue a public 'director warning' which, if ignored, automatically converts into a court disqualification order

questions.explanation: s.842 CA 2014 empowers the CEA to apply directly to court for disqualification orders (and restriction orders under s.819). This is a standalone power independent of liquidator applications. It enables the CEA to pursue disqualification against directors of solvent companies or in cases where no liquidator exists.

questions.question: A director of a company is convicted of insider trading under Irish market abuse legislation. The conviction is on indictment. Can the court make a disqualification order under s.839 CA 2014 arising from this conviction?

options.A: No — disqualification under s.839 is limited to offences under the Companies Acts; market abuse convictions are governed by separate legislation
options.B: Yes — s.839(1) includes conviction on indictment for indictable offences involving fraud or dishonesty in relation to a company, and insider trading clearly involves dishonesty in the context of company affairs
options.C: Only if the conviction results in a custodial sentence exceeding 12 months
options.D: No — a separate application must be made under the Market Abuse Regulation to seek disqualification

questions.explanation: s.839(1)(a) CA 2014 covers conviction on indictment for offences involving fraud, dishonesty, or breach of duty in relation to a company. Insider trading involves the dishonest exploitation of confidential information in relation to a company's securities — this falls squarely within the dishonesty ground and supports a disqualification application.

questions.question: Fyffes plc v DCC plc [2007] IESC was a landmark Irish case involving market abuse (insider dealing). While primarily a market abuse case, what significance does it have in the context of director disqualification?

options.A: The Supreme Court held that insider dealing by a director constitutes a Category 1 offence requiring automatic disqualification without court application
options.B: The Supreme Court found that share dealing using price-sensitive information constituted dishonest conduct — conduct of this kind would ground a disqualification application under s.839(1)(a) as it involves dishonesty in relation to a company
options.C: The case established that the CEA (then ODCE) had no jurisdiction to investigate market abuse offences as these were exclusively within the Financial Regulator's remit
options.D: The Supreme Court held that directors of publicly listed companies are held to a higher standard of conduct and face automatic disqualification on any conviction

questions.explanation: Fyffes v DCC plc [2007] IESC is relevant to disqualification because it established that exploiting price-sensitive information constitutes dishonest conduct. While the case was primarily a civil market abuse case, conduct of this quality — using a director's privileged access to company information for personal gain — would form a strong basis for disqualification under s.839(1)(a) CA 2014.

questions.question: A director of a company that was subject to examinership and subsequently wound up argues that their conduct during the examinership process should be evaluated separately from the period before examinership. Is this a valid approach under the restriction/disqualification regime?

options.A: Yes — the court must assess each period in isolation; conduct before examinership cannot aggravate findings for the post-examinership period
options.B: No — the court takes a holistic view of the director's entire conduct in relation to the company, including the period preceding examinership, during examinership, and after
options.C: Yes — examinership creates a 'clean slate' and directors cannot be restricted or disqualified for conduct predating the examiner's appointment
options.D: Only conduct within the 12-month look-back period under s.818 is relevant; earlier conduct is inadmissible

questions.explanation: Courts apply a holistic assessment of the director's entire conduct in relation to the company's affairs, including the period before examinership and the causes of insolvency. Examinership does not create a 'clean slate'; pre-examinership decisions that contributed to financial collapse remain relevant to the honesty and responsibility assessment.

questions.question: Re Mitek Holdings Ltd [2010] IESC is a significant Supreme Court authority on director accountability. What was the central issue addressed, and what principle did the court affirm?

options.A: Whether the courts had jurisdiction to make both a restriction and a disqualification order against the same director — the Supreme Court held that both may be made cumulatively
options.B: Whether directors of a parent company could be restricted or disqualified for the conduct of subsidiaries — the court held that a director is responsible for the conduct of all companies within their group oversight
options.C: Whether the standard of 'acting responsibly' required directors of insolvent companies to consider the interests of creditors — the Supreme Court affirmed that this duty arises and failure to do so supports restriction
options.D: Whether a shadow director could be subject to restriction proceedings — the Supreme Court held that only de jure directors fall within the s.819 regime

questions.explanation: In Re Mitek Holdings [2010] IESC, the Supreme Court affirmed that 'acting responsibly' under the restriction regime requires directors of insolvent companies to have regard to creditor interests as the financial position deteriorates. Failure to consider creditor interests when acting was relevant evidence of irresponsibility supporting restriction.

questions.question: A person who has been disqualified under s.839 CA 2014 wishes to seek leave to act as a director of a specific company notwithstanding the disqualification. What mechanism is available, and what must be shown?

options.A: There is no mechanism for relief — disqualification under s.839 is absolute and cannot be modified once made
options.B: The person may apply to the court under s.847 CA 2014 for leave to act, satisfying the court that it is appropriate in all the circumstances for leave to be granted
options.C: The person may apply to the CEA for an administrative waiver — if granted, the court disqualification is suspended
options.D: Leave may be granted only by the Court of Appeal; the High Court has no jurisdiction to modify a disqualification order once sealed

questions.explanation: s.847 CA 2014 provides a mechanism for a disqualified person to apply to court for leave to act in a specific capacity notwithstanding the disqualification order. The court has discretion to grant such leave on terms it thinks fit. This is a narrow safety valve for exceptional circumstances.

questions.question: What is the Register of Disqualified Persons, and what obligations arise when a disqualification order is made?

options.A: The Register of Disqualified Persons is maintained by the CRO; the court notifies the CRO within 21 days of making a disqualification order for entry on the register
options.B: The Register is maintained by the Registrar of Companies; the disqualified person must personally register their disqualification within 30 days or commit a further offence
options.C: The Register of Disqualified Persons is maintained by the CRO under s.846 CA 2014, and the court clerk must forward details of every disqualification order to the Registrar for entry
options.D: There is no public register of disqualified persons — disqualification orders are private court records not accessible to the public

questions.explanation: s.846 CA 2014 provides for a Register of Disqualified Persons maintained by the CRO. On making a disqualification order, the court is required to forward the order to the Registrar for registration. The register is publicly accessible, enabling due diligence by creditors and counterparties before dealing with persons in corporate roles.

questions.question: DPP v Quirke [2009] IEHC was an Irish criminal prosecution relevant to company law enforcement. What was its significance in the context of director accountability?

options.A: The court held that the ODCE (now CEA) had no power to prosecute directors for common law fraud — only the DPP could bring such prosecutions
options.B: The case involved a director convicted of fraudulent trading under what is now s.722 CA 2014, illustrating that fraudulent trading can give rise to both criminal liability and provides grounds for disqualification under s.839(1)(a)
options.C: The court held that all directors of a company convicted of a regulatory offence are automatically jointly and severally liable for the company's fine
options.D: The case established that s.839 disqualification cannot be based on regulatory offences; only common law crimes ground a disqualification application

questions.explanation: DPP v Quirke [2009] IEHC involved criminal prosecution of a director for fraudulent trading under the Companies Acts. The case is significant because fraudulent trading conviction on indictment falls within s.839(1)(a) CA 2014 as an indictable offence involving dishonesty in relation to a company — providing concurrent grounds for a disqualification application by the CEA.

questions.question: A person subject to both a restriction order (s.819) and a disqualification order (s.839) acts as a director of a company that has the required minimum paid-up capital under s.820. Does compliance with the capital requirement protect them from breaching the disqualification order?

options.A: Yes — satisfying the s.820 minimum capital requirement is a complete defence to any alleged breach of both the restriction and disqualification orders
options.B: No — the minimum capital requirement is a qualification for restricted persons under s.820, but a disqualification order under s.839 is an absolute prohibition on acting as director. Compliance with s.820 does not permit a disqualified person to act as director
options.C: Only if the disqualification order was made before the restriction order — in that case, the restriction order takes priority and the capital requirement applies
options.D: Yes — where a company meets the minimum capital requirement, any person may act as director regardless of any prior court order

questions.explanation: The minimum capital requirement under s.820 is a qualifying condition for restricted (not disqualified) persons. A disqualification order under s.839 is a complete prohibition — there is no capital-based escape route. A disqualified person commits a criminal offence by acting as director of any company, regardless of its capitalisation.

questions.question: The court is considering whether to make a disqualification order against a director who was not involved in fraud but who, over a period of six years, was director of four companies that all became insolvent, accumulating aggregate creditor losses of €2.5 million. What ground under s.839 CA 2014 is most applicable?

options.A: s.839(1)(a) — conviction on indictment for a dishonesty-related offence
options.B: s.839(1)(b) — the director's conduct makes them unfit to be concerned in the management of a company, having regard to the aggregate pattern of failures across multiple insolvent companies
options.C: s.839(1)(c) — persistent default in relation to relevant requirements under the Companies Acts
options.D: s.839 does not apply — the director must first have been convicted of a criminal offence before disqualification can be sought

questions.explanation: s.839(1)(b) CA 2014 allows disqualification where the court is satisfied that the person is unfit to be concerned in the management of a company. A pattern of insolvencies across multiple companies, aggregating substantial creditor losses, provides compelling evidence of management unfitness — even without dishonesty: Business Communications v Baxter [1995].

questions.question: A disqualification order is made against a director for 8 years. Three years later, the director establishes that their role in the company was minimal, that they cooperated with investigators, and that they have since completed corporate governance training. They apply to have the order varied. What is the court's approach?

options.A: The court cannot vary a disqualification order once made — the only recourse is an appeal within the standard time limits
options.B: The court may, under s.847 CA 2014, grant leave to act in a specific capacity — but this grants limited permission rather than varying the order. The order itself may be appealed to the Court of Appeal
options.C: The court has an inherent jurisdiction to reduce the disqualification period where the disqualified person demonstrates rehabilitation and changed circumstances
options.D: The court may reduce the period to five years if the disqualified person passes a mandatory corporate governance examination administered by the Law Society

questions.explanation: A disqualification order once made is not routinely varied by the original court. s.847 CA 2014 provides the mechanism for seeking leave to act in specific circumstances. Separately, the order may be appealed. The courts are generally reluctant to vary disqualification orders on the basis of post-order rehabilitation, as this would undermine their deterrent and protective function.
